name: Retool Workflows API
what_it_does: Retool Workflows is a low-code workflow automation engine with a REST API for triggering, managing, and monitoring automated workflows — enabling programmatic invocation of Retool-built automations from external systems and AI agents.
use_cases: Triggering Retool Workflows from external events or agents via webhook or REST call
Orchestrating multi-step data operations (database queries, API calls, transformations) through Retool's visual workflow engine
Building approval workflows in Retool and invoking them programmatically from business process automation
Scheduling and monitoring data pipeline workflows with Retool's built-in scheduler
Connecting AI agent outputs to internal Retool tools for human-in-the-loop review and action
not_for: High-frequency event processing at scale — Retool Workflows is for business process automation, not stream processing
Organizations not using Retool as their internal tooling platform
Complex ETL pipelines where dedicated tools (Airflow, dbt, Fivetran) are more appropriate
best_when: Your team already uses Retool for internal tools and you need agents to trigger workflows, pass data into Retool-managed processes, or receive results from Retool automations.
avoid_when: You need a standalone workflow engine without Retool's broader platform dependency, or you need enterprise-scale throughput beyond Retool's workflow execution limits.

auth.notes: Retool API uses personal or service account API tokens passed as Bearer tokens. Workflow webhooks can be configured with optional secret validation. No granular OAuth scopes — tokens have broad Retool account access. Organization-level tokens recommended for automation.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Free tier: 5 users, limited workflow runs per month
pricing.notes: Workflows is included in all Retool plans. Run limits and execution time limits vary by plan. Self-hosted Retool removes per-run limits but requires infrastructure management.

performance.rate_limits: Workflow execution concurrency and monthly run limits vary by plan

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: REST API errors return JSON with message fields. Workflow execution failures surface errors in the Retool UI log but are not always returned synchronously in the trigger API response — agents may not know a workflow failed until they poll for status.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Workflow triggers are not idempotent — each API call creates a new execution. Agents must implement deduplication at the application level.
known_agent_gotchas: Workflow trigger via REST API is typically fire-and-forget — execution result not returned synchronously; agents must poll or use webhook callbacks for results
Workflow execution logs are in the Retool UI, not accessible via API — debugging failures requires human inspection
API tokens are user-scoped, not workspace-scoped — avoid using personal tokens for automation; create service accounts
Retool Workflows run limits vary by plan and reset monthly — hitting the limit silently fails or queues executions
Self-hosted vs cloud Retool has different API endpoint structures — community examples may not be portable
Workflow versioning is done in the UI; agents triggering workflows always run the current deployed version with no version pinning via API

security_score_components.security_notes: Bearer token with no granular scopes is a broad credential. Retool has SOC2 compliance. Workflow secrets can be stored in Retool's secret store rather than hardcoded. Self-hosted deployment provides stronger data isolation. Webhook secret validation available but optional.
